title: Born in West Virginia
dek: Ashley Matheny was born in a small town along the Ohio River in West Virginia.
narrative: Growing up along the Ohio River, water skiing, canoeing, and camping meant her passion for nature ran deep. This early connection to water would shape her entire career trajectory.
mapCaption: Born in West Virginia along the Ohio River.

title: Ohio State University
dek: Earned a Bachelor of Science in Civil Engineering from The Ohio State University.
narrative: As an undergraduate at Ohio State, Matheny immersed herself in measuring water use by trees in a section of Michigan forest under environmental stress. Combining her passions for engineering and nature, this project would evolve into her master's and doctoral research.
mapCaption: Matheny earned her BS in Civil Engineering from Ohio State University.

title: Doctoral research in ecohydrology
dek: A Ph.D. in Civil, Environmental, and Geodetic Engineering from Ohio State, focused on forest transpiration and plant hydrodynamics.
narrative: Ashley Matheny's trajectory was built on rigorous scientific foundation. Earning a Ph.D. from Ohio State University under advisor Gil Bohrer, she established deep technical expertise in understanding how trees use water.

Her doctoral work combined field measurements with numerical modeling to answer questions about how disturbances like drought affect vegetation-climate feedbacks. This scientific grounding proved crucial — her research on tree water storage and hydraulic strategies would become foundational to improving climate models.
proofPoints: 2014: Outstanding Student Paper Award from American Geophysical Union.
2015: Presidential Fellowship from Ohio State University.
2016: Dissertation on plant hydrodynamic modeling completed.
mapCaption: Ph.D. formation at The Ohio State University in Civil, Environmental, and Geodetic Engineering.

title: Postdoctoral research at Ohio State
dek: Postdoctoral Research Scientist in Civil, Environmental, and Geodetic Engineering at Ohio State University, August 2016 to August 2017.
narrative: The postdoctoral year extended her research depth, incorporating detailed tree hydrology data into computer models of water and bioactive element cycling between earth and atmosphere. This work set the stage for her faculty career that followed.
mapCaption: Postdoctoral researcher at Ohio State — deepening expertise before joining UT Austin.

title: Building an ecohydrology research program at UT Austin
dek: Assistant Professor at the Jackson School of Geosciences since Fall 2017 — establishing a research program focused on vegetation water use and land-atmosphere interactions.
narrative: Joining the University of Texas at Austin placed Matheny within a world-class earth sciences program with access to diverse field sites from temperate forests to arid landscapes to coastal mangroves.

In the competitive academic research environment, Matheny built a program combining cutting-edge field measurements with innovative modeling approaches to understand how plants influence water and carbon cycles.
proofPoints: 2017: UT Rising STARs Faculty Award recognizes early career promise.
2020: G. Moses and Carolyn G. Knebel Distinguished Teaching Award.
Multiple graduate students and postdocs join the lab to study ecohydrology.
mapCaption: Matheny joined UT Austin's Jackson School of Geosciences in Fall 2017.

evidenceProfile.strength: Primary university URLs support faculty narrative

title: Expanding research across diverse ecosystems
dek: Field campaigns spanning Michigan forests, Texas Hill Country, New Mexico watersheds, and Gulf Coast mangroves.
narrative: From 2017 to 2021, Matheny methodically expanded her field research footprint. Projects spanned temperate deciduous forests in Michigan, semi-arid juniper-oak systems in central Texas, high-elevation watersheds in New Mexico's Valles Caldera, and coastal mangrove ecosystems along the Texas Gulf Coast.
These diverse field sites allowed her to test hypotheses about plant water use strategies across climatic gradients, from water-abundant to water-limited environments.
proofPoints: Michigan: Long-term sap flux monitoring in red oak, white pine, and red maple.
Texas: Semi-arid forest water use in Ashe juniper and live oak.
New Mexico: Groundwater-surface water interactions in montane watersheds.
Gulf Coast: Mangrove hydrodynamics and salinity stress responses.
mapCaption: Research sites span diverse North American ecosystems.

title: NSF CAREER Award: Understanding biomass hydraulic capacitance
dek: Prestigious five-year NSF CAREER grant to develop novel sensors and models for measuring water storage in trees.
narrative: In 2021, Matheny secured a prestigious NSF CAREER Award, providing five years of funding to establish a strong research and educational program. The project focuses on understanding how trees store water in their biomass — acting as 'water towers' that buffer against drought.

The research creates novel sensors to directly measure water content at different depths within trees, integrating field data with models and NASA satellite products to better predict droughts, tree mortality, and forest fire likelihood.
proofPoints: Five-year NSF funding for sensor development and modeling.
Collaboration with Pete Marchetto (Conservify) on prototype sensors.
Field sites in Michigan temperate forests and Texas semi-arid systems.
Educational component includes immersive field experience videos in English and Spanish.
mapCaption: NSF CAREER Award supports research program based at UT Austin.

title: Exploring halophyte hydrodynamics at the terrestrial-aquatic interface
dek: DOE and ARC grants support mangrove ecosystem research in Texas, Australia, Panama, and Abu Dhabi.
narrative: Matheny's team secured grants from the U.S. Department of Energy and Australian Research Council to study how mangrove ecosystems respond to changes in salinity and atmospheric conditions.

This work extends plant hydraulics models to include osmotic potential — capturing how salt-tolerant plants manage water stress. The research spans four continents, adding vegetation hydraulics and eddy covariance monitoring sites to track mangrove forest responses to climate change.
proofPoints: DOE and ARC funding for multi-site mangrove research.
Collaborations with Los Alamos National Lab, Princeton, Khalifa University, and Monash University.
Field sites in Texas, Australia, Panama, and Abu Dhabi.
Development of halophyte-capable plant hydraulics models.
mapCaption: Mangrove research spans Texas Gulf Coast and international sites.

title: Recognition for leadership in ecohydrology
dek: Outstanding Early Career Award from the American Meteorological Society and continued research impact.
narrative: In 2022, Matheny received the American Meteorological Society's Outstanding Early Career Award in Agricultural and Forest Meteorology for 'leadership and excellence in the study of vegetation hydrodynamics and its role in the surface-atmosphere exchange of water.'

This recognition, combined with her NSF CAREER Award and distinguished teaching honors, establishes her as a leading voice in understanding how vegetation influences water and carbon cycles in a changing climate.
proofPoints: 2022: AMS Outstanding Early Career Award.
2021: NSF CAREER Award.
2020: Distinguished Teaching Award from Jackson School.
2017: UT Rising STARs Faculty Award.
Extensive publication record in top journals.
mapCaption: Recognition for research excellence based at UT Austin.
